section.title: The Triumphal Entry and the Temple
section.sectionRationale: The King arrives at His city. Jesus enters Jerusalem on a colt to shouts of Hosanna, then acts as Lord of the temple, cursing a fruitless fig tree and cleansing the temple courts, two signs of judgment on a fruitless religious system. The withered tree the next morning interprets the cleansing, and when the leaders challenge His authority, Jesus silences them with a question about John. The final confrontation in Jerusalem has begun.
section.thesis: Jesus enters Jerusalem as the promised King and comes to His temple as its Lord, and finding it fruitless like the leafy fig tree, He enacts God's judgment on empty religion, calling instead for a house of prayer, faith, and forgiveness, while His authority, questioned by the leaders, is shown to be from heaven.
section.frameNote: Four beats: the triumphal entry; the cursing of the fig tree and the cleansing of the temple; the withered fig tree with teaching on faith and prayer; and the challenge to Jesus' authority.

units.label: The triumphal entry
units.note: Blessed is He who comes in the name of the Lord

verses.text: As they approached Jerusalem and came to Bethphage and Bethany at the Mount of Olives, Jesus sent out two of His disciples
and said to them, “Go into the village ahead of you, and as soon as you enter it, you will find a colt tied there, on which no one has ever sat. Untie it and bring it here.
If anyone asks, ‘Why are you doing this?’ tell him, ‘The Lord needs it and will return it shortly.’”
So they went and found the colt outside in the street, tied at a doorway. They untied it,
and some who were standing there asked, “Why are you untying the colt?”
The disciples answered as Jesus had instructed them, and the people gave them permission.
Then they led the colt to Jesus and threw their cloaks over it, and He sat on it.
Many in the crowd spread their cloaks on the road, while others spread branches they had cut from the fields.
The ones who went ahead and those who followed were shouting: “{{hosanna|Hosanna}}!” “Blessed is He who comes in the name of the Lord!”
“Blessed is the coming kingdom of our father David!” “Hosanna in the highest!”
Then Jesus entered Jerusalem and went into the temple courts. He looked around at everything, but since it was already late, He went out to Bethany with the Twelve.

units.label: The fig tree and the temple
units.note: a house of prayer for all the nations

verses.text: The next day, when they had left Bethany, Jesus was hungry.
Seeing in the distance a fig tree in leaf, He went to see if there was any fruit on it. But when He reached it, He found nothing on it except leaves, since it was not the season for figs.
Then He said to the tree, “May no one ever eat of your fruit again.” And His disciples heard this statement.

When they arrived in Jerusalem, Jesus entered the temple courts and began to drive out those who were buying and selling there. He overturned the tables of the money changers and the seats of those selling doves.
And He would not allow anyone to carry merchandise through the temple courts.
Then Jesus began to teach them, and He declared, “Is it not written: ‘My house will be called a house of {{proseuche|prayer}} for all the nations’? But you have made it ‘a den of robbers.’”
When the chief priests and scribes heard this, they looked for a way to kill Him. For they were afraid of Him, because the whole crowd was astonished at His teaching.
And when evening came, Jesus and His disciples went out of the city.

units.label: The withered fig tree and faith

verses.text: As they were walking back in the morning, they saw the fig tree withered from its roots.
Peter remembered it and said, “Look, Rabbi! The fig tree You cursed has withered.”
“Have {{pistis|faith}} in God,” Jesus said to them.
“Truly I tell you that if anyone says to this mountain, ‘Be lifted up and thrown into the sea,’ and has no doubt in his heart but believes that it will happen, it will be done for him.
Therefore I tell you, whatever you ask for in prayer, believe that you have received it, and it will be yours.
And when you stand to pray, if you hold anything against another, forgive it, so that your Father in heaven will forgive your trespasses as well.”

units.label: The challenge to Jesus' authority
units.note: by what authority are You doing these things

verses.text: After their return to Jerusalem, Jesus was walking in the temple courts, and the chief priests, scribes, and elders came up to Him.
“By what {{exousia|authority}} are You doing these things?” they asked. “And who gave You the authority to do them?”
“I will ask you one question,” Jesus replied, “and if you answer Me, I will tell you by what authority I am doing these things.
John’s baptism, was it from heaven or from men? Answer Me!”
They deliberated among themselves what they should answer: “If we say, ‘From heaven,’ He will ask, ‘Why then did you not believe him?’
But if we say, ‘From men’...” they were afraid of the people, for they all held that John truly was a prophet.
So they answered, “We do not know.” And Jesus replied, “Neither will I tell you by what authority I am doing these things.”

hosanna.gloss: Hosanna, from the Hebrew hoshia-na, save now or save, we pray, a cry from Psalm 118 that had become a shout of praise. The crowds hail Jesus with the pilgrim psalm, welcoming Him as the one who comes in the name of the Lord and the bearer of David's kingdom.

proseuche.gloss: Prayer. Quoting Isaiah, Jesus declares that the temple was meant to be a house of prayer for all the nations, a place of access to God for the whole world. The traders had made the court of the Gentiles a marketplace, robbing the nations of that access.

pistis.gloss: Faith, trust in God. In light of the withered tree and the coming end of the temple system, Jesus calls His disciples to have faith in God, a confident, forgiving, prayerful trust that lays hold of God's power for what accords with His will.

exousia.gloss: Authority, the right to act. The leaders demand to know by what authority Jesus cleanses the temple and teaches. His authority is heavenly, of a piece with John's, and their refusal to answer honestly exposes their own lack of any authority to judge Him.

basic.context: Jesus enters Jerusalem riding a colt, and the crowds throw down cloaks and branches, shouting Hosanna and hailing the coming kingdom of David. He enters the temple, looks around, and leaves. The next day, hungry, He curses a fig tree that has leaves but no fruit. Then He enters the temple and drives out the traders, declaring that God's house was meant to be a house of prayer for all nations, not a den of robbers. The next morning the fig tree is withered to its roots, and Jesus teaches His disciples to have faith in God, to pray believing, and to forgive. When the leaders demand to know by what authority He acts, He asks whether John's baptism was from heaven; unable to answer without exposing themselves, they say they do not know, and Jesus refuses to answer them.
basic.hermeneutics: The fig tree and the temple cleansing interpret each other: Mark wraps the cleansing inside the fig tree story so we see the temple, like the tree, as all show and no fruit, under God's judgment. Jesus comes as King and as Lord of the temple. The teaching on faith, prayer, and forgiveness points beyond the doomed temple to a new access to God. The authority question shows the leaders' bad faith.
basic.originalLanguages: The crowds cry hosanna, save now, from Psalm 118. Jesus says the temple should be a house of proseuche, prayer, for all nations. He calls for pistis, faith, in God. And the leaders demand by what exousia, authority, He acts.
basic.typology: The King on the colt fulfils Zechariah's prophecy of the humble King coming to Zion. The fruitless fig tree is a prophetic sign-act of judgment on Israel's leadership. The cleansed temple and its coming end point to Jesus Himself as the true temple and to the access to God He opens for all nations.
basic.christ: Jesus is the King who comes in the name of the Lord, the Lord who comes to His temple in judgment, and the one whose authority is from heaven. He is the true meeting place between God and people.
basic.god: God comes to inspect His house and finds it fruitless. God desires His house to be a place of prayer for all peoples, and He judges the religion that has become a racket. God calls His people to faith, prayer, and forgiveness.
basic.crossReferences: Zechariah 9 foretells the humble King on a colt. Isaiah 56 and Jeremiah 7 lie behind the temple saying. Psalm 118 supplies the Hosanna.
basic.oneStory: The King long promised rides into His city, and the Lord comes to His temple, but He finds it fruitless and enacts its judgment, as He did the fig tree. The old house of stone, meant to be a house of prayer for all nations, has failed; Jesus Himself will become the true temple through His death and resurrection, opening access to God for the whole world.

context.literary: Chapter 11 opens the Jerusalem section that runs to the cross. Mark uses his signature sandwich: the fig tree cursing, verses 12 to 14, brackets the temple cleansing, verses 15 to 19, and is resolved by the withered tree, verses 20 to 25, so the tree interprets the temple. The authority challenge begins a series of temple confrontations that continue in chapter 12.
context.historical: Pilgrims flooded Jerusalem at Passover, and messianic hopes ran high. Spreading cloaks and branches and shouting Psalm 118 was a royal welcome. The temple's outer court, the court of the Gentiles, had become a market for sacrificial animals and currency exchange, crowding out the very space where non-Jews could pray. Challenging a teacher's authority in the temple was a formal, public confrontation.
context.canonical: The entry fulfils Zechariah 9:9, your King comes to you, humble and riding on a colt. The temple saying joins Isaiah 56:7, a house of prayer for all nations, with Jeremiah 7:11, a den of robbers, casting Jesus as a prophet announcing judgment on the temple as Jeremiah did. The withered fig tree recalls prophetic images of fruitless Israel, Jeremiah 8:13 and Hosea 9:10, and points to the true temple, John 2:19-21.

hermeneutics.authorIntent: To present Jesus entering Jerusalem as the promised King and coming to His temple as its Lord, to enact through the fig tree and the cleansing God's judgment on fruitless religion, and to point beyond the doomed temple to faith, prayer, and the new access to God that Jesus Himself provides.
hermeneutics.descriptionVsPrescription: The entry and the temple actions describe unique, prophetic events in Jesus' mission. The teaching on faith, prayer, and forgiveness is prescriptive, though the promises about moving mountains must be read within God's will and Jesus' larger teaching on prayer, not as a blank cheque. The judgment on the temple is a specific historical act with abiding warning against empty religion.

debates.question: Why did Jesus curse a fig tree for having no fruit when it was not the season for figs?

views.label: A prophetic sign-act against fruitless Israel
views.case: The leafy but fruitless tree pictures the temple and its leaders, all outward show and no fruit; Jesus curses it as an enacted parable of the judgment about to fall, not out of mere frustration.

views.label: A lesson in genuine versus pretended fruitfulness
views.case: A fig tree in full leaf normally bears early fruit; its bare branches were a false advertisement, symbolizing a religion that promises much and yields nothing, which God will not tolerate.

debates.bearing: Both readings agree the tree is a living parable: God comes seeking fruit, and finding only leaves, He judges the pretense, a warning framed around the temple cleansing it encloses.

debates.question: What is the meaning of Jesus' promise about moving mountains by faith?

views.label: Confidence in God's power for His purposes
views.case: In context, this mountain may point to the temple mount whose end Jesus foretells; faith lays hold of God to accomplish what seems impossible when it accords with His will, as the withering tree has just shown.

views.label: A call to believing, forgiving prayer
views.case: Jesus binds the promise to prayer and forgiveness, verses 24 and 25; the emphasis falls on wholehearted trust in God and a heart cleared of grudges, not on treating faith as a technique to command God.

debates.bearing: Both hold that the point is confident trust in the living God, exercised in prayer and forgiveness, not a formula guaranteeing whatever we desire regardless of God's will.

study.originalLanguages: The Passover crowds cry hosanna, from the Hebrew save now of Psalm 118, welcoming Jesus as the King who comes in the name of the Lord. In the temple He quotes Isaiah: God's house was to be a house of proseuche, prayer, for all the nations, a place of access for the whole world, now choked into a robbers' den. Interpreting the withered tree, Jesus calls His disciples to pistis, faith, in God, expressed in believing, forgiving prayer that points beyond the doomed temple to a new nearness to God. And when the leaders demand by what exousia, authority, He acts, His counter-question about John exposes that their real problem is not ignorance but unwillingness to submit to heaven's authority in Him.

typology.type: The humble King on a colt
typology.fulfillment: Jesus fulfilling Zechariah's promise of Zion's King coming lowly and riding on a colt, Zechariah 9:9 and Matthew 21:4-5.
The judgment on fruitless Israel and its temple, a prophetic sign fulfilled in the temple's coming destruction, Jeremiah 8:13 and Mark 13:2.

typology.type: The temple as house of prayer for all nations
typology.fulfillment: Christ Himself as the true temple and the access to God opened for every nation, John 2:19-21 and Ephesians 2:18-22.

study.christ: Jesus enters Jerusalem as the long-promised King, welcomed with the psalm of the coming Son of David, yet He rides not a war-horse but a humble colt, the King who reigns through the cross. He comes to the temple as its Lord to inspect and to cleanse, and finding it fruitless like the leafy fig tree, He pronounces judgment on empty religion and reclaims God's house as a place of prayer for all peoples. His authority, which the leaders dare not name, is from heaven, of a piece with John's. And in all this Jesus points beyond the doomed house of stone to Himself: the true temple, torn down and raised in three days, who opens access to God for the whole world.
study.god: God visits His house to seek fruit and finds only leaves, and He will not endure a religion of outward show that yields nothing. God's design for the temple was breathtakingly wide, a house of prayer for all the nations, and its corruption into a marketplace robbed the peoples of access to Him. God calls His people instead to faith, to believing prayer, and to forgiveness, the marks of true nearness to Him. And God's authority stands behind His Son, so that to refuse Jesus is to refuse heaven itself, as the leaders' evasive answer reveals.

crossReferences.note: Rejoice, your King comes to you, righteous and humble, riding on a colt, fulfilled in the triumphal entry.
My house will be called a house of prayer for all nations, the purpose Jesus reasserts in cleansing the temple.
Has this house become a den of robbers? Jeremiah's temple warning that Jesus applies to His own day.
Destroy this temple and in three days I will raise it up; He spoke of the temple of His body, the true meeting place with God.

study.oneStory: The King so long awaited rides into His city, and the Lord comes at last to His temple, but He comes to judge as well as to save. Finding the house of God fruitless, all leaves like the withered fig tree, He enacts its judgment and reclaims God's purpose that it be a house of prayer for all nations. The old house of stone has failed, but Jesus Himself is the true temple, and through His coming death and resurrection He will open access to God for the whole world. The confrontation that will lead to the cross has begun, and even now the answer to the leaders' question is plain: His authority is from heaven.

quiz.q: What did the crowds' welcome of Jesus into Jerusalem signify?

options.text: They hailed Him as the King coming in the name of the Lord, welcoming the coming kingdom of David.
options.note: Mark 11:9-10; the Hosanna and Psalm 118 shouts are a royal, messianic welcome.

options.text: They welcomed a respected teacher passing through the city.
options.note: Their cries of Hosanna and kingdom of David hailed far more than a teacher; they welcomed the messianic King.

options.text: They celebrated a prophet entering for the Passover feast.
options.note: There is truth here, but the shouts specifically acclaim the coming Davidic kingdom, not merely a prophet.

options.text: They demanded He lead a revolt against Rome.
options.note: The crowds praised Him with a pilgrim psalm; Jesus came as a humble King, not a military rebel.

quiz.q: How do the fig tree and the temple cleansing relate?

options.text: The fruitless leafy tree is a sign of the fruitless temple, both under God's judgment for show without substance.
options.note: Mark wraps the cleansing inside the fig tree story so each interprets the other.

options.text: They are two unrelated events that happened close together.
options.note: Mark deliberately intertwines them so the tree interprets the temple's judgment.

options.text: Both show Jesus acting in judgment on empty religion.
options.note: True, but it should note the pointed link: the barren tree pictures the fruitless temple.

options.text: Both show Jesus was simply hungry and frustrated.
options.note: The cursing and cleansing are prophetic sign-acts of judgment, not mere frustration.

quiz.q: What did Jesus say God's house was meant to be?

options.text: A house of prayer for all the nations, which they had made a den of robbers.
options.note: Mark 11:17 joins Isaiah 56:7 and Jeremiah 7:11.

options.text: A place of sacrifice for the people of Israel.
options.note: Sacrifice had its place, but Jesus stressed prayer and access for all nations, which the market had displaced.

options.text: A place of worship where God is honoured.
options.note: True but general; Jesus specifically said a house of prayer for all the nations, now made a robbers' den.

options.text: A marketplace to serve the many pilgrims.
options.note: The opposite; Jesus drove out the market that had corrupted the house of prayer.

quiz.q: What does Jesus teach about faith and prayer after the fig tree withers?

options.text: To have faith in God, to pray believing, and to forgive others so the Father will forgive us.
options.note: Mark 11:22-25 binds faith, believing prayer, and forgiveness together.

options.text: That faith can accomplish anything we wish for, without condition.
options.note: The promise is set within trust in God's will and joined to forgiveness, not an unconditional blank cheque.

options.text: To trust God confidently in prayer and to forgive others.
options.note: Correct in substance, but it should also name believing that you have received what accords with God's will.

options.text: That prayer is unnecessary if you have enough faith.
options.note: Jesus commands believing prayer; faith and prayer go together, not one instead of the other.

quiz.q: How did Jesus respond when the leaders demanded to know His authority?

options.text: He asked whether John's baptism was from heaven or men, exposing their bad faith when they refused to answer.
options.note: Mark 11:29-33; their evasion revealed they would not submit to heaven's authority.

options.text: He plainly told them His authority came from God.
options.note: He did not answer directly; He exposed their unwillingness to acknowledge the truth about John and Himself.

options.text: He answered their question with a question that unmasked their hypocrisy.
options.note: Accurate, but it should name the specific question about John's baptism and their fear-driven evasion.

options.text: He refused to speak to them at all.
options.note: He did engage them, posing a searching question before declining to answer their challenge.

questions: The crowds shouted Hosanna, yet many would soon call for His crucifixion. Where might your praise of Jesus be shallow or conditional, and how can it become true allegiance?
Jesus found leaves but no fruit. If He came looking for fruit in your life today, what would He find beneath the outward show?
God's house was meant to be a house of prayer for all nations. How does that shape the way you think about prayer and about welcoming outsiders to God?
Jesus joined believing prayer with forgiving others. Is there someone you are holding something against that is hindering your prayers?
The leaders would not honestly face the question about John. Where are you tempted to dodge the truth about Jesus rather than submit to His authority?

translationNotes.note: Hosanna transliterates the Hebrew hoshia-na, save now or save, we pray, from Psalm 118:25-26; a plea for salvation that had become a shout of joyful praise at the pilgrim feasts.

The BSB does not print a verse 26, since the words some manuscripts add, about the Father not forgiving if we do not forgive, are drawn from Matthew 6:15 and absent from the earliest witnesses here; the numbering skips from 25 to 27.
John's baptism, was it from heaven or from men originally used a dash for the pause; rendered here with a comma. From heaven means from God, the source of true authority behind both John and Jesus.

meta.notes: BSB text fetched this session from bible.helloao.org for Mark 11:1-33. Footnote markers, line breaks, and poem markers were dropped when assembling verse text. Verse 26 is absent from the BSB main text and is noted; verses run 1 to 25 then 27 to 33. The em dash in verse 30 was converted to a comma. No em or en dashes remain. Sermon left as none; no expositor sermon URL verified this session.
